answer: Wheat grows best in cool, dry climates (15–20°C). Sow between October–December. Use certified seeds at 100kg/ha. Apply 120kg N, 60kg P, 40kg K per hectare. Irrigate at crown root initiation, tillering, jointing, flowering, and grain filling stages. Harvest when moisture is below 14%. Common diseases: rust, smut — use resistant varieties.

Rice requires 20–35°C temperature and 150–200cm rainfall. Transplant 25-day seedlings at 20×15cm spacing. Apply 120kg N/ha in 3 splits. Maintain 5cm standing water during vegetative stage. Drain 10 days before harvest. Watch for blast disease, brown planthopper, and stem borer.

Tomato grows best at 20–27°C. Transplant 4-week seedlings. Space at 60×45cm. Apply 150kg N, 60kg P, 60kg K per hectare. Stake plants at 30cm height. Irrigate every 5–7 days. Common problems: early blight, late blight, fruit borer. Use drip irrigation to reduce disease.

Onion requires well-drained loamy soil. Transplant at 15×10cm spacing. Apply 100kg N, 50kg P, 50kg K per hectare. Avoid waterlogging. Stop irrigation 2 weeks before harvest. Cure bulbs in shade for 7–10 days before storage. Common pest: thrips — use spinosad spray.

Cotton needs 21–30°C and 60–100cm rainfall. Sow in May–June. Space at 90×60cm. Apply 180kg N, 90kg P, 90kg K per hectare. Critical irrigation at flowering and boll formation. Major pests: bollworm, whitefly, aphids. Use Bt cotton varieties for bollworm resistance.

Sugarcane grows best in tropical climate. Plant setts at 75–90cm row spacing. Apply 250kg N, 60kg P, 60kg K per hectare. Irrigate every 7–10 days. Earthing up at 60 and 120 days. Harvest at 10–12 months. Common diseases: red rot, smut — use disease-free setts.

Maize grows at 21–27°C. Sow at 60×25cm spacing. Apply 120kg N, 60kg P, 40kg K per hectare. Critical irrigation at knee-high, tasseling, and grain filling. Harvest when husk turns brown. Common pests: fall armyworm, stem borer — use coragen spray.

Potato grows best at 15–20°C. Plant certified seed tubers at 60×20cm. Apply 180kg N, 80kg P, 100kg K per hectare. Hill up soil at 30 days. Irrigate every 7–10 days. Stop irrigation 2 weeks before harvest. Major disease: late blight — spray mancozeb preventively.

Integrated Pest Management (IPM): 1) Monitor fields weekly, 2) Use yellow/blue sticky traps, 3) Release natural enemies like Trichogramma, 4) Apply neem oil (5ml/L) as first line, 5) Use chemical pesticides only when pest crosses economic threshold. Rotate pesticide groups to prevent resistance.

Balanced fertilization: Always do soil test first. General NPK for cereals: 120:60:40 kg/ha. Split nitrogen — 1/3 basal, 1/3 at tillering, 1/3 at panicle initiation. Add 5 tonnes FYM/ha before sowing. Use micronutrients (zinc sulfate 25kg/ha) if deficiency observed. Avoid excess nitrogen — causes lodging and disease.

Efficient irrigation tips: Drip irrigation saves 40–60% water. Sprinkler saves 25–30%. Irrigate in early morning or evening. Use tensiometers to measure soil moisture. Critical stages: germination, flowering, grain filling. Avoid waterlogging — causes root rot. Mulching reduces evaporation by 30%.

Healthy soil management: Test soil every 3 years. Ideal pH: 6.0–7.5 for most crops. Add organic matter (FYM, compost, vermicompost) to improve structure. Avoid over-tilling. Use green manure crops (dhaincha, sunhemp) to fix nitrogen. Soil organic carbon above 0.5% is essential for good yield.

Disease management: Use certified disease-free seeds. Follow crop rotation (3-year cycle). Apply fungicides preventively during humid weather. Common fungicides: mancozeb (contact), propiconazole (systemic). Remove and destroy infected plant parts. Maintain proper spacing for air circulation.

Weather-smart farming: Check 7-day forecast before sowing and spraying. Avoid pesticide application 24 hours before rain. During drought, use mulching and deficit irrigation. Install rain gauge to track local rainfall. Consider crop insurance (PM Fasal Bima Yojana) for weather risks. Sow drought-tolerant varieties in low-rainfall areas.

Organic farming practices: Use vermicompost (2–3 tonnes/ha), FYM (10 tonnes/ha), and biofertilizers (Rhizobium, PSB, Azotobacter). Prepare jeevamrit: 10L cow urine + 10kg cow dung + 2kg jaggery + 2kg gram flour in 200L water, ferment 48 hours. Apply 200L/acre every 15 days. Reduces input cost by 40%.

Key government schemes: 1) PM-KISAN — ₹6000/year direct benefit, 2) PM Fasal Bima Yojana — crop insurance at 1.5–2% premium, 3) Kisan Credit Card — crop loan at 4% interest, 4) Soil Health Card — free soil testing, 5) PM Krishi Sinchai Yojana — drip/sprinkler subsidy up to 55%. Contact your local agriculture office or call Kisan Call Centre: 1800-180-1551.

Market information: Check daily mandi prices on eNAM portal (enam.gov.in) or Agmarknet. MSP (Minimum Support Price) is announced by government before each season. Sell through FPOs (Farmer Producer Organizations) for better prices. Avoid distress selling — use warehouse receipt financing. APMC mandis provide transparent auction system.

Farm mechanization: Tractor rental costs ₹800–1200/hour. Use custom hiring centers for affordable access. Key machines: rotavator (soil prep), seed drill (sowing), sprayer (pest control), combine harvester (harvesting). Proper maintenance: change oil every 100 hours, check tire pressure weekly, clean air filter daily during field work.

Post-harvest storage: Dry grain to safe moisture (wheat 12%, rice 14%, maize 12%) before storage. Use hermetic bags (PICS bags) for small quantities. Fumigate with aluminum phosphide tablets in sealed storage. Cold storage for vegetables and fruits. Warehouse Receipt System allows loans against stored produce.

Crop rotation benefits: Breaks pest and disease cycles, improves soil fertility, reduces input costs. Good rotations: Rice-Wheat, Cotton-Wheat, Maize-Potato. Intercropping: Maize+Soybean, Sorghum+Cowpea, Cotton+Groundnut. Legumes in rotation fix 50–100kg N/ha naturally, saving fertilizer cost.

• Pest and disease management
• Fertilizer and irrigation advice
• Government schemes and subsidies
• Market prices and storage

Just ask me anything about farming!

question: What crops grow in black soil?
answer: Black soil is ideal for crops like cotton, soybean, groundnut, sunflower, and pulses due to its high moisture retention and nutrient content.

question: What crops grow in black soil?
answer: Black soil is very fertile and retains moisture well. It is best for crops like cotton, soybean, groundnut, sunflower, and pulses. Cotton grows especially well in this soil.

question: Which crops grow in red soil?
answer: Red soil is suitable for crops like groundnut, millets, pulses, tobacco, and cotton. It requires proper irrigation and fertilizers for better yield.

question: What is the best fertilizer for rice?
answer: Rice grows best with a balanced use of nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium (NPK). Urea is commonly used for nitrogen supply, along with DAP for better growth.

question: How to increase crop yield?
answer: Crop yield can be increased by using quality seeds, proper irrigation, balanced fertilizers, pest control, and following crop rotation techniques.

question: What is drip irrigation?
answer: Drip irrigation is a method where water is delivered directly to plant roots, saving water and improving efficiency.

question: How to control pests in crops?
answer: Pests can be controlled using natural pesticides like neem oil, chemical pesticides if needed, and maintaining proper field hygiene.

question: What are Kharif crops?
answer: Kharif crops are grown during the monsoon season (June to October). Examples include rice, maize, cotton, and soybean.

question: What are Rabi crops?
answer: Rabi crops are grown during winter (October to March). Examples include wheat, mustard, barley, and peas.

question: How often should crops be watered?
answer: Watering depends on crop type, soil, and weather. Most crops require watering every 2 to 5 days.

question: What is organic farming?
answer: Organic farming uses natural fertilizers like compost and avoids chemical pesticides, improving soil health and producing safe food.

question: What is PM Kisan scheme?
answer: PM Kisan is a government scheme that provides Rs.6000 per year to farmers in three installments for financial support.

question: Which crop is best for summer season?
answer: Crops like maize, sunflower, watermelon, and vegetables grow well during summer.

question: How to improve soil fertility?
answer: Soil fertility can be improved by adding organic matter, using compost, crop rotation, and balanced fertilizers.

question: What is crop rotation?
answer: Crop rotation is growing different crops in the same field in different seasons to maintain soil nutrients and reduce pests.

question: How to get better price for crops?
answer: Farmers can get better prices by selling in government mandis, using online platforms, and storing crops properly.
